coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of blue-photoluminescent graphene quantum dots/polystyrenic anion-exchange resin for Fe(III) detection
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Wenjun
Gan, Jie
coredata.dc:description: A novel solid fluorescent sensor with millimeter size, based on graphene quantum dots/polystyrenic anion-exchange resin (GQDs/PS-AER) was obtained for the detection of Fe3+. The linear response range of Fe3+ was obtained from 1μM to 7μM and the detection limit was as low as 0.65μM. In addition, the sensor could be regenerated by adding complexing agent EDTA and be separated by using simple filtration.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Fluorescent sensor
Fe3+ detection
Polystyrenic anion-exchange resin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ions with GQDs/PS-AER

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of Fe3+ detection based on graphene quantum dots/PS-AERs.

(a):UV–vis absorption (Abs, blue) and emission spectrum (red) of the GQDs at 337nm excitation wavelength and excitation spectrum (black) of GQDs at 438nm emission wavelength. Inset: Photograph of the GQD aqueous solution taken under 365nm UV light. (b): emission and excitation spectrum of the GQDs/PS-AER. (c) emission of the ARE (black line) and GQD/PS-ARE (red line) with excitation at 360nm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
FT-IR spectra of as-prepared GQDs (a), PS-AER and GQDs/PS-AER (b).
SEM images of the surfaces of the anion-exchange resins without GQDs adsorption (a) and with GQDs adsorption (b). (c) TEM image of GQDs.
(a) Fluorescent emission spectra of the solid GQD/PS-AER at different concentrations of Fe3+. (b) The relationship between the PL intensity and the Fe3+ concentration.
Fluorescent emission spectra of the PS-AER at different concentrations of Fe3+.
Selectivity of the Fe3+ sensor. All competing ion solutions were 1μM.
The PL recovery of GQD/PS-ARE in the presence of EDTA (0.02M).
Fluorescent emission spectra of the recovered GQD/PS-AER at different concentrations of Fe3+ (a–e represent the 5 cycles respectively).
Fluorescence quenching rate of recovered GQD/PS-AER at different cycles and concentrations of Fe3+.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of blue-photoluminescent graphene quantum dots/polystyrenic anion-exchange resin for Fe(III) detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin 300130, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel solid fluorescent sensor with millimeter size, based on graphene quantum dots/polystyrenic anion-exchange resin (GQDs/PS-AER) was obtained for the detection of Fe3+. The linear response range of Fe3+ was obtained from 1μM to 7μM and the detection limit was as low as 0.65μM. In addition, the sensor could be regenerated by adding complexing agent EDTA and be separated by using simple filtration.

ce:section.ce:para: Fe3+ ion is one of the essential metals in biological systems and plays crucial roles in cellular metabolism[1]. In addition, it serves as a fundamental structure of hemoglobin, myoglobin, haemenzymes and many cofactors involved in enzyme activities [2]. Exceeding concentration level of Fe3+ can be a potential health hazard, as reported [1]. Therefore, the identification and quantification of Fe3+ in clinical, medicinal, environmental and different industrial samples are of great importance.

Up to now, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are discovered as a class of zero-dimensional graphitic nanocrystals with tiny sizes of only several nanometers, strong quantum confinement [3–5] and edge effects [6,7]. GQDs possess the advantages of lower toxicity [8], stronger luminescence and higher photostability in comparison to conventional semiconductor quantum dots, thus making them promising in the fields of sensors [9–11], bioimaging [12], environmental monitoring [13,14] and so on. Recently, GQDs have been used as efficient sensors for Fe3+ detection. A facile and direct fluorescence sensor for the detection of Fe3+ ions was reported by Chen’s group [15], which could effectively and selectively detect Fe3+ concentrations as low as 90nM. In another work, N-doped graphene quantum dots were prepared from the hydrothermal carbonization of citric acid for the detection of Fe3+ in water, reported by Tran Van et al. [16]. Generally, GQDs were fabricated from larger graphene sheets by top-down approaches like acidic oxidation [17–19].The nanometer-sized GQDs were obtained by filtering and dialysis processes, which is time-consuming and complicated for more efficient separation of GQDs. Furthermore, the difficulty of separation made it hard to reuse the nano-sized GQDs.

Herein, we report development of a big-size sensor for Fe3+ detection with the use of GQDs as an effective sensing platform (Scheme 1 ). GQDs were synthesized through an acid oxidation method and simply adsorbed by PS-AERs with the granularity between 0.3mm to 1.2mm. Then the as-prepared GQDs-adsorbed resins were further used for the detection of Fe3+ and the detection limit could be reached to 0.65μm. The large particle size could be even more conducive to the separation than microspheres. Specially, the fluorescence could be recovered by adding EDTA solution and separated by simple filtration.

Natural graphite powder, Nitric acid (37%), sulfuric acid (98%), sodium carbonate were obtained from Tianjin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Anion-exchange resin(201×7, granularity, 0.3–1.2mm), Aqueous solutions of K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Co2+ and Fe3+ were prepared from nitrate salts; solutions of Cd2+ and Ni2+ was prepared from chlorate salts; Fe2+ solution was prepared from ferrous sulfate and used immediately.

The UV–vis spectrum was recorded at room temperature on a Phenix UV1900 in the wavelength range of 260–600nm. Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) in the range of 4000–400cm−1 was measured on KBr disks utilizing a Bruker Tensor27 spectrometer (Germany). Fluorescence measurements were carried out with a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The morphology of the PS-AER bead surface was imaged using scanning electron microscopy (NanoSEM 450). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were taken on a JEOL-2100F transmission electron microscope.

GQDs were obtained following a method below. Graphite (1.000g) was added into a mixture of concentrated sulfuric acid (48mL) and nitric acid (16mL)[18]. The reaction mixture was subjected to ultrasonic treatment for 3h. The mixture was then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (150mL) and heated for 24h at 120°C. The as-prepared products contained yellow transparent suspension and black precipitates. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was diluted with 400mL deionized water, and then the black precipitates were filtered. The yellow transparent suspension was neutralized (ca. pH 7) with sodium carbonate. Then the suspension was evaporated under reduced pressure and filtered to remove the precipitated inorganic salts. The filtrate was collected.

Four 50mL flasks with 2mL of 200mg/L as- prepared GQD aqueous solution were shaken with 0.300g resin at room temperature under constant stirring at 150rpm. Consequently, the as-prepared GQDs were adsorbed on resin beads. Then the GQDs-adsorbed resins were filtrated and collected for Fe3+ detection.

Stock standard solutions 0.01M Fe(III) were prepared by dissolving an appropriate amount of FeCl3·6H2O in water and adjusting the volume to 100mL in a volumetric flask. It was further diluted from 10−3 to 10−6 M stepwise and other metal ion solutions were prepared in the same way before use. The fluorescent intensity of the GQDs/PS-AER was recorded, after different concentrations of Fe3+ aqueous solution were added into the same amount of GQD/PS-AER respectively and the mixed solutions were equilibrated for 10min, the GQDs/PS-AER were filtrated and collected for recording the fluorescent intensity again.
Fe3+-quenched GQD/PS-AER were shaken together with EDTA solution for 5min, it was then filtered and reused for Fe3+ detection. The regeneration and reuse cycles were repeated for up to five cycles.

Blue color was exhibited by the GQDs under ultraviolet radiation (λ=365nm). The UV–vis absorption spectrum and photoluminescent (PL) emission spectra of GQDs in water were shown in Fig. 1 a. The UV–vis absorption peak was around 360nm, which was assigned to the π–π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains. On the excitation of 337nm, the PL spectrum showed a strong peak at 438nm. The emission and excitation spectrum of the GQD/PS-AER were shown in Fig. 1b. The PL emission spectra of the solid of PS-AER and GQD/PS-ARE with excitation at 360nm were shown in Fig. 1c; the PS-AER and GQD/PS-AER showed emission at 478 and 468nm, respectively, the 10nm blue-shift of the PL emission. Moreover, the PL intensity of GQDs/PS-AER was considerably greater than that of PS-AER. The functional groups on the GQDs surface were presented in Fig. 2 . The spectrum of GQDs showed broad absorption bands at 3463cm−1 corresponding to hydrogen-bonded OH, the bands at around 1637cm−1 were attributed to the carboxyl groups. Therefore, we speculated that GQDs exhibited the main sheet structure of graphene, with rare carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups remaining on the sheets. The presence of O-rich groups made the GQDs soluble in aqueous medium and provided additional advantages for further foundation to meet various requirements. TEM was also employed for the characterization of the obtained GQDs. Although high quality TEM images are difficult to be obtained due to the low electro contrast between the GQDs and the carbon coated TEM grids, GQDs with a size of less than 10nm can still be observed in Fig. 3 c. These observations are consistent with those prepared from previous reports [20].

The characteristics of GQDs/PS-AER and pure PS-AER were investigated using attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR). Fig. 2b shows that the characteristic peaks at 1638cm−1 and 1564cm−1 corresponding to CC on the benzene of GQDs/PS-AER have a little blue shifts and the intensities are obviously enhanced. Which indicates composites have formed mainly through π–π stacking interaction between PS-AER and GQDs.

The surface morphology of bare resin and GQDs-adsorbed resin were shown in Fig. 3a and b. The surface of bare resin is smooth and the surface of GQDs-adsorbed resin become rougher, which indicates that the GQDs could be adsorbed on the surface of the PS resin. The polystyrenic structure and the ionization of molecular carboxyl, phenolic hydroxyl groups on the surface of the GQDs contributed to its electrostatic negativity, which made it easily absorbed onto PS-AERs with positively charged groups[21] (such as polystyrenic matrix and functional groups of quaternary ammonium) via π–π stacking interactions and electrostatic attraction [22,23]. Furthermore, we have studied the effect of macroporous acrylic acid anion exchange resin (D311) on the adsorption of GQDs. Unfortunately, GQDs cannot be adsorbed on the surface of the D311 resin. This result indicates that the interactions of GQDs and PS-AERs is mainly via π-π stacking.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ions with GQDs/PS-AER
ce:section.ce:para: To study the sensitivity of GQDs/PS-AER for Fe3+ detection, different concentrations of Fe3+ were added to the GQD/PS-AER and the fluorescence intensities were measured. The PL spectra of GQD/PS-AER solid in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ ions at 343nm was shown in Fig. 4 a, which indicated that PL intensity of the GQD/PS-AER was sensitive to Fe3+ concentration and decreased with increase of Fe3+ concentration. It is believed that Fe3+ ions could form metal hydroxides by specially coordinating with the abundant hydroxyl groups on the surface of GQDs/PS-AER. The as-formed metal complexes would facilitate charge transfer from GQDs/PS-AER to Fe3+, resulting in the PL quenching behavior [24]. The relationship between the PL intensity and the Fe3+ concentration was shown in Fig. 4b.The linear correlation coefficient (R) was 0.971, which indicated a good linear relationship for solid luminescent materials. The k was the slope of the calibration curve. The limit of detection of Fe3+ was estimated to be 0.65μM based on 3S0/K in which the S0 was the standard deviation of the corrected blank measurements (n=7) [10]. Furthermore, we studied the sensitivity of PS-AER for Fe3+ detection. It is shown in Fig. 5 that the fluorescence intensity decreased with the increasing concentration of Fe3+ at 343nm and the detection limit was around 500μM which was lower than GQD/PS-AER.

At the same time, the luminescent responses of GQD/PS-AER toward aqueous solutions of various metal cations were studied. The various fluorescence intensity ratio (I/I0) of the GQD/PS-AER in the absence or presence of various metal ions at the concentration of 1μM were particularly investigated (Fig. 6 ). I0 and I were the fluorescence intensity at 343nm in the absence and presence of ions, respectively. The fluorescence intensity was sensitively quenched by Fe(III) ions, whereas it could hardly get quenched by other metal ions, such as, K(I), Na(I), Mg(II), Ca(II), Cu(II), Zn(II), Cd(II), Fe(II), Ni(II), Co(II) and so on. This result is probably due to the faster chelating process of Fe3+ ions with GQDs/PS-AER through “O” in comparison with other metal ions. These results might provide a new pathway for the detection of Fe (III) ion.

To evaluate the possibility of reusing the GQD/PS-AER, desorption studies were carried out (Fig. 7 ). The addition of EDTA to GQD/PS-AER quenched by Fe3+ leaded to the recovery of PL emission. It demonstrated that the Fe3+ loaded on the GQD/PS-AER could be desorbed in EDTA solution. The recovered sensor was then reused for Fe3+ detection and the florescence response of the recovered sensor to Fe3+ was shown in Fig. 8 . Obviously, the PL intensity of recovered GQD/PS-AER still kept on decreasing with increasing of Fe3+ concentration as before. Furthermore, we investigated the Fluorescence quenching rate of recovered GQD/PS-AER, which shown in Fig. 9 . In the five cycles, the quenching rates of recovered GQD/PS-AER which quenched by 1μM Fe3+ remained stable. In addition, the quenching rates of recovered GQD/PS-AER which quenched by 10μM, 100μM, 1000μM Fe3+ revealed a similar result. These results suggested that it could be used as a cost-effective reusable absorbent for easy, convenient, and efficient detection of Fe3+.

In summary, the fluorescent sensor of GQDs/PS-AER with millimeter size was successfully prepared for the detection of Fe3+ ion. GQDs with stable blue emission were first adsorbed onto the surface of polystyrenic anion-exchange resin. As a solid fluorescent detector, The big-sized sensor exhibits excellent analytical performance for determination of Fe3+, ranging from 1.0×10−6 to 7×10−6 M with a detection limit of 0.65μM. Moreover, the GQD/PS-AER composites can be used as a cost-effective reusable fluorescence sensor by adding EDTA. This proposed method provides a new pathway for the detection of Fe3+ due to the excellent fluorescence stability, low cost and simple operations.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2016.02.248.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
